1: bōqēq (H1238) — to empty, drain, lay waste; from a root meaning to pour out completely. The verb conveys total depletion rather than mere destruction. bôlēq (H1110) — to lay waste, make desolate; the near-synonym in the same verse intensifies the completeness. YHWH empties the earth of its human order and then distorts its surface — the cosmic judgment is both demographic and physical.
2: The social leveling formula: priest/people, master/servant, mistress/maid, buyer/seller, lender/borrower, creditor/debtor — six pairs spanning the entire social hierarchy. The reversal is grammatical equality: kāʿam kakkōhēn ("as with the people, so with the priest"). Social structures that organize human community are dissolved by the coming judgment — no status exempts.
3: The repetition of the root bwq twice in one verse — hēbôq tibboq — is an infinitive absolute construction that intensifies: "will be completely/utterly emptied." Similarly hbz tibbaz — "plundered, it will be plundered." The double infinitive absolute construction is Isaiah's way of expressing absolute certainty with rhetorical force. YHWH has spoken this word (peh Yhwh dibber).
4: ʾābelâ nābĕlâ hāʾāreṣ — "the earth mourns/dries up, the world withers"; ʾābal (H56) can mean both to mourn (as people do) and to dry up (as plants do) — the deliberate double meaning personifies the earth as a mourner while describing its literal ecological collapse. mārôm — the height/heavens; the cosmic mourning reaches vertically through all creation.
5: ḥillĕpû tôrâ — "transgressed the laws/instruction" (tôrâ without the article, suggesting law in general, not specifically Moses); ḥālĕfû ḥōq — "violated the statute/decree"; hēpĕrû bĕrît ʿôlām — "broken the everlasting covenant." The bĕrît ʿôlām most likely refers to the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16), which is with "all flesh" and "the earth" — explaining why creation itself is cursed. Violation of the universal covenant produces universal ecological judgment.
6: ʾālâ (H423) — oath/curse; the covenant curse (qĕlālâ) has consumed (ʾāklâ) the earth. Those who live on it are pronounced guilty (nĕʾāšĕmû, held to account). The diminishing of the inhabitants — wayyiśrāpû yōšĕbê-ʾāreṣ ("the inhabitants of the earth are scorched/burned") — may be the result of drought or plague, both covenant curse instruments (Deuteronomy 28:22-24).
7: ʾābel tîrôš — "the new wine mourns/withers" — the same ʾābal double meaning as Isaiah 24:4 (mourns/withers). The festive grape harvest becomes an occasion of grief. ʾumllâ gephen — "the vine languishes/is sick" (ʾāmal, H535 — to be faint, sick, languid). The joy of the wine culture is eliminated as a specific sign of covenant curse — wine gladdens the heart (Psalms 104:15); its absence means cursed desolation.
8: šābat (H7673) — ceased/stopped; the root of Sabbath. The joyful tambourines (tōp), the noisy revelers (šaʾôn ʿallîzîm), and the joyful harp (kinnôr) all fall silent. In the ancient world, music, drinking, and celebration were inseparable from civic and cultic life — their cessation signals total social collapse.
9: Strong drink (šēkār) becomes bitter to those who drink it — the reversal of the expected pleasure. They no longer sing to wine (lōʾ yāšîrû layyāyin). The drinking-song culture of the ANE was part of how people celebrated life, harvest, and victory; its silencing is both literal (no harvest) and metaphorical (nothing to celebrate).
10: qiryat-tōhû — "city of chaos/formlessness" — the word tōhû (H8414) is the same root as in Genesis 1:2 (the primeval chaos before creation: tōhû wābōhû). The city, which represents the height of human civilizational achievement, is reduced to primeval formlessness. šubar kol-bayit — "every house is broken/shut up" — the city is physically impassable.
11: The crying in the streets is for wine (ʿal-hayyāyin) — the same wine that mourned in Isaiah 24:7. The cry is specifically the absence of the expected substance of celebration. ʿārbâ kol-śimḥâ — "all joy has grown dark/has set" (ʿārab, to grow dark, as the sun at evening). gālâ mĕśôś hāʾāreṣ — "the joy of the earth has gone into exile."
12: šûʾâ (H7722) — desolation/ruin; the city is left as a wasteland (nišʾar bāʿîr šammâ). The city gates (šaʿar) are battered to ruins (yuškat šaʾar lĕtōhû). Gates in the ANE were the judicial and commercial center of city life — their destruction means the end of all public order and commerce.
13: The remnant imagery: kĕnōqep zayit — "like the gleaning of the olive" (nōqep, the beating/shaking of the olive tree to dislodge the last olives); kĕʿôlēlôt — "like gleanings/after-pickings" after the grape harvest. The survival of a remnant is compared to the few olives or grapes left after thorough harvesting — the remnant is intentionally left, not forgotten. Compare Isaiah 17:6.
14: The remnant raises its voice — yiśśĕʾû qôlām — and sings for joy (yārōnnû). The geographical direction is significant: they sing "from the west" (mimmāʿărāḇ). The cosmic scope of judgment (Isaiah 24:1-13) produces a cosmic scope of praise — the remnant in the distant west responds to YHWH's majesty (gĕʾôn Yhwh). This is not a nationalistic but a universal gathering.
15: bāʾûrîm — "in the east/in the fires" — highly debated. LXX reads "in the light" (ʾôr); MT likely means "in the regions of light" (east). bĕʾiyyê hayyām — "in the coastlands of the sea." The universal praise comes from east and west simultaneously — the people of YHWH are scattered and gathered from every direction.
16: mikkĕnap hāʾāreṣ — "from the wing/edge of the earth" — the extremities of the inhabited world. The songs of praise are for the ṣaddîq ("Righteous One" — may be a title for YHWH or the messianic agent). The prophet's response: rāzî lî — "my secret/my leanness to me!" — a cry of personal grief or exhaustion at what he has seen. bōgedîm bāgādû — "the treacherous have been treacherous" — the world's faithlessness continues even as judgment falls.
17: paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ — the three "p" words: terror, pit, and snare — a rhetorical triple alliteration in Hebrew that is untranslatable but memorable. Flight from one danger leads directly to another (the one who flees the terror falls into the pit; the one who escapes the pit is caught in the snare). This is the inescapability of the cosmic judgment.
18: ʾarûbbôt mimmārôm niptāḥû — "the windows of heaven are opened" — the language of the Flood (Genesis 7:11). The cosmic catastrophe reactivates the Flood imagery, confirming that the bĕrît ʿôlām of Isaiah 24:5 is indeed the Noahic covenant. The foundations of the earth (môsĕdê ʾāreṣ) shake (yirʿāšû) simultaneously — judgment attacks both from above and below.
19: The threefold verb assault: rōʿâ rōʿāʿâ hāʾāreṣ ("the earth is utterly broken") — rāʿaʿ (H7489) means to be shattered/shaken violently; pôr hitpōrĕrâ ("completely dissolved/split apart"); môṭ hitmoṭĕṭâ ("moved violently/shaken to the core"). Three different verbs of earth-shaking each intensified by their own infinitive absolute — the rhetorical force mimics the seismic event.
20: nûaʿ tānûaʿ hāʾāreṣ kaššikkôr — "the earth staggers like a drunkard" — the same verb nûaʿ (H5128) used for swaying/staggering in intoxication. The earth is compared to a drunk man (šikkôr) and to a hammock (mĕlûnâ, temporary shelter) — both images convey instability. Its transgression (pišʿāh) weighs it down; it falls and will not rise again.
21: yipqōd Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt — "YHWH of hosts will visit in judgment" (pāqad, H6485 — to attend to, to visit for judgment or blessing depending on context). The object is twofold: the "host of the exalted ones on high" (ṣĕbāʾ hammārôm bammārôm — probably celestial/spiritual powers) and the kings of the earth (malkê hāʾădāmâ). The cosmic dimension of the judgment includes both supernatural and human authority structures.
22: yē ʾaspû ʾăsēpâ — "they will be gathered, gathered together" (double infinitive absolute for certainty) — like prisoners into a pit (bôr) and shut up (sāgar) in a prison (kelēʾ). After many days they will be visited (yuppāqēdû) — the same pāqad as in Isaiah 24:21, now for release or further judgment. The delay suggests a temporary holding before final judgment.
23: bôš halbānâ ûpĕrâ hahammâ — "the moon (lĕbānâ, the white one) will be ashamed and the sun (ḥammâ, the hot one) will be abashed" — when YHWH reigns on Mount Zion (mālak Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt bĕhar ṣiyyôn) and in Jerusalem before his elders (ziqnāyw). The cosmic lights are eclipsed by the glory of YHWH's royal presence — the image of divine enthronement that renders all other light secondary.
